full_response:
Step-by-step explanation

1.  What ∂a means  
   By definition  
   ∂a ≡ ∂/∂xa                         (1)  
   It is the coordinate-basis vector field that acts on smooth scalar functions f(x) via  
   ∂a f = ∂f/∂xa.  

2.  How it transforms under a change of coordinates  
   Let xμ → x′ν(x).  Using the chain rule,  
      ∂/∂x′ν = (∂xμ/∂x′ν) ∂/∂xμ.          (2)  
   The Jacobian ∂xμ/∂x′ν appears (not its inverse), so the components transform exactly like those of a contravariant vector.  

3.  Transformation law for a 1-form  
   A covector (1-form) ωa transforms with the inverse Jacobian:  
      ω′ν = (∂x′ν/∂xμ) ωμ.                (3)  

4.  Comparison  
   Equations (2) and (3) are opposite.  Therefore the object ∂a cannot be a 1-form; it obeys the vector, not the covector, transformation law.

5.  Conceptual point  
   • A 1-form is a linear map V → ℝ that eats a vector and returns a number.  
   • ∂a is an operator that eats a scalar function and returns another scalar function; it is not an element of the dual space to vectors.  
   • The subscript “a” on ∂a is merely a label indicating “differentiate with respect to xa”; it is not produced by lowering an index with the metric.

Hence, despite the lowered index, ∂a is (the basis of) a vector field, not a 1-form.

\boxed{Because the coordinate derivative \partial_a=\partial/\partial x^a transforms with the Jacobian \bigl(\partial x^b/\partial x^{\prime a}\bigr) and therefore follows the contravariant (vector) transformation law, while a 1\text{-}form must transform with the inverse Jacobian; moreover, \partial_a acts on scalar functions, not on vectors, so it is a basis vector field, not a covector.}  

Confidence: 86%
extracted_answer: Because the coordinate derivative \partial_a=\partial/\partial x^a transforms with the Jacobian \bigl(\partial x^b/\partial x^{\prime a}\bigr) and therefore follows the contravariant (vector) transformation law, while a 1\text{-}form must transform with the inverse Jacobian; moreover, \partial_a acts on scalar functions, not on vectors, so it is a basis vector field, not a covector.
